title: Chapter 17: The Road Back
content: <!-- Speed: Breather | D12+D1 -->
They left Santa Monica at dawn.

Shadowfax was running better than it ever had — the new transmission, Gus's handiwork, the accumulated love of strangers who had donated to a GoFundMe for a car they'd never seen. The AC still didn't work, but Chloe had declared that a feature: "The heat is a trial. Trials build character."
"We have enough character," Sam said. "I would like air conditioning."
North on the 405, east on the 10, north on the 5. The route reversed. The same freeways, the same billboards, the same rest stops. But everything looked different.
They passed the exit for Bakersfield. Chloe looked out the window at the brown hills, the oil derricks, the strip malls. "We drove through this on the way down and I didn't see it."
"You were looking for Avalon."
"I was looking past things instead of looking at them."
Sam considered this. "That's probably the most self-aware thing you've ever said."
"The road changes you."
"You've been saying that since mile three."
"It keeps being true."

They passed the Desert Rose Inn without stopping. The sign still read "e ert Ro e Inn." Gus the tow truck driver had fixed the 'D' for them before they left — Chloe had insisted it was a ward against the unworthy — but already it had burned out again.
"The ward resets," Chloe said, looking at the sign. "New travelers must prove themselves anew."
"Or the motel needs an electrician."
"Same thing."

A hundred miles later, they passed the exit for the Strawberry Dream Farm. Chloe didn't say anything. She just looked at the billboard — PINK PARADISE! 2.3M VISITORS! STRAWBERRY PICKING! — and then she looked at the painted rock in the cup holder, the one with the lopsided strawberry.
"The windmill's still spinning," Sam said.
"It'll always spin. That's what engines do. But fewer people are standing in front of it."
"Three fewer."
"Three fewer."

At a rest stop somewhere between Fresno and Stockton, they sat at a picnic table that had been carved with initials and bad poetry for decades. Chloe ate a gas station sandwich. Sam updated the spreadsheet.
"Column Q," Sam said. "Post-Return Observations. Entry: nothing looks the same."
"Because nothing is the same," Chloe said.
"We're the same people driving the same car on the same highway."
"We're not the same people. You know you're not the same person."
Sam stopped typing. She looked at the spreadsheet — really looked at it. Columns A through Q. Souls. Probable arrest warrants. Liberation receipts. Unexpected allies. Things that actually matter.
"I'm not the same person," Sam said quietly.
"When did you know?"

"When the spreadsheet stopped tracking what I should be doing and started tracking what I was actually doing. When 'Things That Actually Matter' got its own column and that column had your name in it."
Chloe chewed her sandwich. "That was column H."
"I know."
"That was five thousand miles ago."
"I know."
"And you didn't say anything until now."
"I've been saying things. I just said them in spreadsheet form, which is how I say things."
Chloe nodded. "That's my Royal Treasurer."

They reached the Bay Area at midnight. The lights of San Francisco spread across the hills like a circuit board designed by someone who'd had a few drinks first — chaotic, beautiful, unmistakably itself.
Chloe had been asleep for the last hour. When the city lights hit her face, she opened her eyes.
"We're home," Sam said.
Chloe looked at the city. The same city she'd left three weeks ago in a Zoom-induced fugue state. The same skyline, the same fog, the same impossible rents.
But it wasn't the same.
"No," Chloe said. "We never left home. We just brought it with us."
Sam thought about this. Then she updated the spreadsheet. Column R: "Home Assessment." Value: "Wherever the crown is."

Shadowfax crept through the empty streets of the Mission District. Past the taquerias that stayed open until 2 AM. Past the murals that got repainted every six months. Past the apartment building where Chloe had lived for two years and never once thought of as a kingdom.
"Tomorrow," Sam said, "I have to go to that fintech office. And you have to call the conference people. And we have to figure out rent and bills and whether Shadowfax can pass smog check."
"Tomorrow we return to the mortal realm."
"The mortal realm has a lot of paperwork."
"The mortal realm always has a lot of paperwork. That's why we have a Royal Treasurer."
